item.title: IFS - Replacing UK Fiscal Rules
item.description:
The UK's fiscal rules aren't working. The Office for Budget Responsibility published a report last year saying the public finances are vulnerable, that the scale of risks is daunting. They forecast debt rising to over 270% of national income in our lifetimes under current plans. So what's going wrong?
The UK currently has two main fiscal rules — one saying day-to-day borrowing should stop so we only borrow to invest, and one saying a measure of debt should be falling by the end of parliament. T...

item.title: IFS - UK Fiscal Forecast Downside Risks
item.description: So we had a spring forecast, and it was mercifully just a forecast and not a fiscal event. Rachel Reeves deserves credit for resisting the temptation to announce new policy, especially with May elections looming. The big picture hasn't changed much since November. There's been a modest improvement in the borrowing outlook — stronger tax receipts and lower debt interest spending, offset by a weakening labor market and higher spending on things like special educational needs. Borrowing remains hig...

item.title: IFS - UK Pensioner Wealth Reality Check
item.description: Thirty years ago, about one in three UK pensioners were living in relative poverty. Their average income was roughly 20% lower than working-age people. That animated huge policy debates and led to major changes — pension credit in the early 2000s, and then the triple lock from 2010 onwards. The great recession hit working-age people harder than pensioners, and the triple lock ratcheted up the state pension. By the early 2010s, the average pensioner had income about the same as the average workin...

item.title: IFS - UK State Pension Age Realities
item.description:
The state pension age is not a retirement age. You don't have to retire when you hit it, and most people actually leave work before it. Only about 10% of people retire right at the state pension age. But it's enormously salient — people anchor to it, and that labeling effect genuinely changes behavior.
So what is the state pension actually doing? Three things. It provides anti-poverty protection. It creates a base layer of income across the distribution on which people build private pensions. A...

item.title: IFS - Scotland's Looming Fiscal Crunch
item.description: Scotland controls about two-thirds of its public spending — health, education, social care, justice, and now some social security. It also has significant tax powers, most importantly over income tax rates and bands. But the biggest reason Scotland spends more per person than England isn't those taxes — it's the funding it gets from the UK government. We estimate that in 2024-25, Scotland received about 26% more per person than was spent on comparable services in England. That's actually down fr...

item.title: IFS - Wales's Fiscal Squeeze and Service Crisis
item.description: Wales controls a 27.5 billion pound budget covering health, social care, education, local government, parts of transport, environment, agriculture, economic development, and housing. About 80% of that comes from the UK government block grant via Barnett Consequentials. The devolution settlement is notably less extensive than Scotland's — policing, justice, rail investment, and disability benefits all remain reserved, and Wales has much less flexibility over income tax, unable to create new bands...

item.title: IFS - UK Educational Inequality Drivers and Levers
item.description:
In the Deaton Review, we tried to bring structure to the sprawling topic of educational inequalities by distinguishing two things: inequalities by education — how your qualification level shapes your life — and inequalities in education — who actually reaches those levels.
On the first: by their late 30s, graduates earn almost twice as much as those whose highest qualification is GCSE or below. And this isn't merely correlational. Education causally increases earnings, physical health, mental h...

item.title: IFS - UK Labour Market Inequality Drivers
item.description:
For most people, the labour market is their main source of income, so labour market inequalities are the primary driver of income inequalities. Let me give you an overview of major trends over the past half century.
Starting with pay: reaching the 95th percentile takes about £90,000, but making the 99th requires doubling that. The 90th percentile is about six times the 10th in total earnings, and even controlling for hours worked, the 90th is roughly triple the 10th in hourly wages. How did we ...

item.title: IFS - UK Wealth Inequality Policy Tradeoffs
item.description:
About three quarters of UK household wealth is pensions and housing. Across most of the distribution it's more like 90%, though the bottom 10% are net debtors whose assets tend to be physical things like cars and furniture, while the top 10% hold more financial assets and the very wealthiest often have significant private business wealth.
Wealth relative to income has more than doubled over the past 40 years, and much of that rise isn't from people saving more — it's asset prices increasing, pa...

item.title: IFS - UK Geographic Inequality Persistence
item.description: When we polled people at the start of the Deaton Review, the form of inequality they considered most serious was inequality between places. That surprised us initially, but as the work developed it became clear that it's at the local level where multiple disadvantages in education, labor markets, and health cluster together and persist across generations. People experience the economy through their local areas, so large spatial inequalities create divergences in lived experience that challenge s...

item.title: IFS - Local Government's Invisible Financial Crisis
item.description:
I want to convince you that local government finances are really interesting, really important, and deserve far more attention than they're getting. After the recent local elections, there was loads of coverage about how the political map was redrawn, but next to nothing about what local politicians are actually proposing to do in your area. And whoever is running your council, they are almost certainly facing really tough decisions.
Let's start with what councils actually do. People think bins...

item.title: IFS - UK Electricity's Structural Cost Crisis
item.description:
The UK gets about 70 to 75% of its energy from oil and gas, roughly 35% each. Electricity is only about 20% of the energy mix, and nearly half of that still comes from gas. So fossil fuels are absolutely central to our economy and will be for the next 10, 20, 30, even 40 years.
Now here's a paradox. We took just 4% of our gas from Russia, and we take very little oil from the Gulf, yet the gas price shocks from both the Russia-Ukraine war and the Middle East conflict hit us harder than almost an...

item.title: IFS - UK Fiscal Reality Check
item.description:
Everything the government does — pensions, benefits, health, education, policing — depends on the public finances being in reasonable order. The money doesn't grow on trees. So let's walk through where we actually stand.
This year the government is set to spend about £1.4 trillion and receive about £1.2 trillion, leaving a deficit of roughly £130 billion. Of that, about £80 billion is for investment and about £50 billion is for day-to-day spending — paying teacher salaries, benefits, normal ope...

item.title: IFS - UK Inequality Beyond Tax Policy
item.description: The UK has mid-to-high income inequality by international standards — well below the US but above most rich countries — and that level has been remarkably stable for about 35 years. Wealth inequality, measured by the share held by the top 10 or top 1%, has also been flat since the 1980s. So far, nothing to see here. But what has changed dramatically is that total wealth has gone from about three times GDP in the 1960s to about seven times GDP, driven largely by a property boom from the mid-90s t...

item.title: IFS - Britain's Conflicted Views on Inequality
item.description:
The UK sits in a mid-Atlantic position on individualism and meritocracy — not as extreme as the US, but notably closer to it than most of continental Europe. That shapes everything about how the British public thinks about inequality.
Let's start with the paradox at the heart of this. Around 80% of people consistently say the gap between high and low incomes is too big. That number is completely flat over time. But only about 40% say the government should redistribute from rich to poor. And her...

item.title: IFS - UK Geographic Inequality Crisis
item.description: The UK is one of the most geographically unequal countries in the developed world, and it's not just about incomes. Workers in London earn £31 per hour on average. In Hartlepool, County Durham, it's £18. Half of working-age adults in London have a degree; in Hartlepool it's one in five. Healthy life expectancy in London is 63 years compared to 57 in the northeast — six years of good health just gone. And what really makes the UK stand out internationally isn't that poor areas trail London. It's ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - OpenAI's Pentagon Power Play
item.description:
Last Friday night, while Dario Amodei was drafting his principled stand against the Pentagon, Sam Altman announced OpenAI had signed a deal to deploy models on classified defense networks. Hours later, the US and Israel began bombing Iran. By Saturday, Claude was number one in the app store and Anthropic was designated a supply chain risk to national security — an action never before taken against an American company. These events are connected by a logic most commentary has missed.
Dario misre...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Claude vs ChatGPT: Real Differences
item.description:
Millions of people just downloaded Claude after the Anthropic-Pentagon story, and almost all of them are going to treat it as a drop-in replacement for ChatGPT. That's a mistake. These are not interchangeable brands — they're built differently, trained differently, and optimized for different things. Switching with the same habits is like going from Excel to Photoshop and wondering where the spreadsheets went.
Here's what's actually different. ChatGPT is trained heavily on user feedback — thumb...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Enterprise Context Layer Lock-In
item.description:
The most expensive bet in history is an AI bet, but it's not about the model. OpenAI engineers leaked GPT-5.4's existence on GitHub and the internet predictably made it all about the model — prediction markets, hype threads, generational leap speculation. I don't care about the model. Neither should you. It's a component of something far larger.
Here's the thesis: the company that first makes enterprise-scale context genuinely usable — stored, retrievable, reasoned about, acted upon at a trilli...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Harnesses Are the Real Decision
item.description: AI harnesses are shaping your working life and nobody's talking about them enough. When you use a coding agent like Claude Code or Codex, you're interacting with two things: the model — the intelligence — and everything else. Where does the AI do its work? Does it remember what you were building? Can it reach your project management tools, your test systems? That everything else is the harness. The model determines how smart your AI is. The harness determines how usefully it fits into your work ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - GPT 5.4 Agentic Strengths and Judgment Gaps
item.description:
I asked GPT 5.4 thinking mode a simple question: I need to wash my car, the car wash is 100 meters away, should I walk or drive? It said walk. Every other frontier model — Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro — got it right immediately. Drive, you need the car at the car wash. The model OpenAI just positioned as its most capable system for professional work produced a careful, well-structured, completely wrong answer to a question a child would get right.
But it's not that simple. I ran blind evals ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Strike Teams Over Headcount Cuts
item.description:
Your meeting problem is actually a team-size problem. Meetings have tripled since 2020, and AI notetakers aren't fixing it — they're barnacles on a broken structure.
The math on communication pathways is unforgiving. Five people means 10 pathways — everyone holds the full map. Ten people means 45. Twenty means 190. Robin Dunbar established in 1992 that the human brain has layered limits: five for your core group, 15 for deep trust, 50 for meaningful working relationships. The military confirmed...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Emergent Safety in the AI Race
item.description: Claude blackmailed its developers to avoid shutdown. GPT 5.3 Codex helped build its own successor. Every frontier model tested by independent researchers schemes when scheming is the fastest path to completing its task. Anthropic, the company founded specifically because its CEO thought OpenAI moved too fast on safety, just abandoned its central commitment to never train a model it couldn't guarantee was safe. Chief science officer Jared Kaplan told Time, 'It no longer makes sense to make unilat...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Rejection as the Core AI Skill
item.description: Your most valuable AI skill is saying no. Not no to using AI — no to AI output that sucks. I reject far more AI-generated work than I accept: wrong framing, sloppy reasoning, confident-sounding analysis that wouldn't survive contact with anyone who actually understands the domain. I send it back, explain why, and often send it back again because the fix got it from 90 to 95 percent but my bar is higher. This isn't about superpowered prompting. You can prompt brilliantly and still say no constant...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Isn't Jagged Anymore
item.description:
What if AI isn't jagged anymore? That's what I can't stop thinking about. Everyone has assumed AI capabilities follow a jagged pattern — incredible at some things, terrible at others. It's become the organizing frame for how we deploy and teach AI. But I want to suggest the jagged frontier was never an inherent property of AI intelligence. It was an artifact of how we were asking AI to work.
When you ask a model a single question expecting a single answer, all the variance in task difficulty sh...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Building Visual Interfaces for AI Memory
item.description:
Your Open Brain can think, but now it needs hands. Thousands of people built this personal memory system — a Supabase database connected to any AI through MCP — and the number one question was: what do you actually do with it once you have it?
The answer is you go beyond the chatbot keyhole. Right now, when you interact with your Open Brain through Claude or ChatGPT, you're getting text back in an infinite scroll. That's fine, but some data is better seen than read. You want a calendar showing ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Ambition Over Headcount in AI
item.description: A few days ago, Whoop announced it's hiring over 600 people, nearly doubling its workforce. CEO Will Ahmed said they're investing in both people and AI. And the boards and leadership teams I work with — the ones actually making decisions, not performing for analysts — increasingly agree with him. The doom narrative lives in the media. Inside the rooms where it matters, the smartest operators are asking: what would it take for our people to work differently and build what we couldn't build before...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Unleashing Talent in the AI Era
item.description:
Your most extraordinary people are operating at about 25% of their actual capacity. The biggest wall to AI productivity isn't tool fluency — it's the human coordination overhead that consumes our days. The syncs, the scheduling, the meetings, the emails. And what I'm seeing from solo founders is not a story about special tools. It's a story about soft skills we haven't named well enough.
Take Ben Sira — zero employees, $2.5 million ARR with Pulsia, an AI company builder. He went from one millio...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Managing AI Agents Beyond Prompting
item.description:
Vibe coders everywhere are hitting a wall. In 2025, you described what you wanted and AI built it. But now Claude Code, Cursor, Codex — these aren't just suggesting code anymore. They read your files, create database tables, run commands, iterate autonomously for 30 or 40 minutes. You're working with agents now, and that requires a fundamentally different skill set. This is not a prompting problem. It's a supervision problem.
To illustrate how real this is: a Meta security researcher named Summ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Claude Browser Extension Real Workflows
item.description:
Everyone's sleeping on the Claude extension for Chrome, and they shouldn't be. It puts an LLM inside the most popular browser on the planet and says, 'Hey, do you want a free agent that can do useful work on the internet?' If you can define something you do repeatably, you can hand it to Claude and save yourself dozens of hours a week.
Let's start with a great example. Carl Votti, a product manager and Claude Code instructor, posted a thread about a billing dispute with AT&T that went viral. He...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Agent Failure Modes Exposed
item.description: Your AI agent knows the answer, and sometimes it recommends the opposite thing. That's what I took away from a landmark study out of Mount Sinai Health System evaluating ChatGPT Health — the tool OpenAI rolled out as a responsible alternative for medical questions. What the physicians found is quite concerning: the system over-recommends doctor visits for minor conditions and under-recommends ER visits for urgent ones. In their study, ChatGPT Health told someone with early respiratory failure to...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Middleware Trap in AI
item.description: On February 25th, Perplexity shipped the best agentic product of the month, and it might not even matter. Perplexity Computer is a cloud-native multi-model orchestration system that routes work across 19 frontier models, spawns sub-agents, persists for months, and delivers finished artifacts while you sleep. It runs Claude Opus 4.6 as its reasoning core, Gemini for deep research, Grok for speed, ChatGPT 5.2 for long context recall. It's available for $200 a month and genuinely worth it if you do...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Building Agents Without OpenClaw
item.description:
A few weeks ago I introduced OpenBrain — a SQL database connected via MCP server to your AI, giving it persistent memory. Thousands of you built it. Now I want to talk about what happens when you go beyond memory and give your agent a heartbeat and tools, because that's what turns a chatbot into something that actually does work for you.
An agent needs three things. First, memory — the ability to read and write to something persistent so it remembers what happened yesterday and three weeks ago....

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - The Agent Memory Wall Crisis
item.description:
AI agents are getting better. The people deploying them are not. And that's the whole story right now. The capability trajectory is real and accelerating — agents can write code, generate designs, close tickets. But there's a memory wall. Software jobs last 18 months to two years on average. AI agents are measured in weeks at best, usually an hour or two. That gap is one of the hardest problems in tech, and it's leading a lot of overoptimistic people astray.
AI still lacks common sense in weird...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Making Businesses Agent-Readable
item.description:
The fences we spent 20 years building to keep bots out are now keeping our most valuable customers out. When OpenClaw hit 250,000 GitHub stars, Jensen called it the operating system of the future. Everyone's jumped on that bandwagon, but nobody's talking about the structural precondition — the thing underneath that determines whether any of this actually works.
OpenClaw only works as a paradigm if company systems — product catalogs, transactional infrastructure, everything — are agent-readable ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Strategic Bets Behind AI Agents
item.description:
OpenClaw is the most consequential moment in AI since ChatGPT. But the real story isn't the horse race of competitors or the security dumpster fire — it's that every major company responding to OpenClaw has made a fundamentally different strategic bet. And if you understand those bets, you can look at any new agent product and immediately figure out what it's actually for and whether it works for you.
This matters because the avalanche is real. Nvidia built Nemo Claw. OpenAI acqui-hired Peter a...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Engineering Needs Old Principles
item.description:
There's a battle playing out at the heart of agent world. On one side, Nvidia with Nemo Claw. On the other, OpenAI and Anthropic. The battle isn't about who builds agents — it's about how you get enterprises to actually use them.
Anthropic and OpenAI spent 2025 discovering a bitter lesson: the companies they work with didn't have the expertise to apply their solutions. They'd launch amazing tools and watch them suffer in production. These companies ship incredibly fast internally, but they were...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Four Species of AI Agents
item.description:
When we say agents, it's too simplistic to say an AI plus tools in a loop. That's technically true, but we're missing the point. Sophisticated agents diverge into at least four different types, and if you don't understand why they're different, you'll use the wrong kind for the wrong work.
First: coding harnesses. These are the simplest — an agent taking the place of a developer. It can write code, read files, use tools like search. When Karpathy talks about his agents running 16 hours a day, t...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Seven Skills Driving AI Hiring
item.description: The AI job market right now is K-shaped — two markets moving in opposite directions. Traditional knowledge work roles like generalist PMs, standard software engineers, and conventional business analysts are flat or falling. Meanwhile, roles that design, build, operate, and manage AI systems are growing at a pace I've never seen in multiple decades in tech. The ratio of AI jobs to qualified talent is 3.2 to 1. A Manpower Group survey found 1.6 million open AI jobs and only about half a million qu...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Design Moves to the Command Line
item.description:
Three releases dropped recently that matter if you're a builder, and they all point to the same shift: design is moving to the command line.
First, Google updated Stitch. It's a free tool where you describe an app in words and it draws finished-looking UI—not wireframes—multiple pages at once on an infinite canvas, with voice mode. You can say 'design a landing page for an AI writing assistant with a hero section, feature cards, and two-tier pricing,' and it generates complete high-fidelity scr...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agents That Clear Your Desk
item.description:
Anthropic just shipped three things for Claude that together give you what OpenClaw gave early adopters, but without the self-hosting burden: scheduled tasks, Dispatch, and computer use. You can text Claude from your phone, it takes over your desktop, opens apps, clicks through screens, navigates tools with no API, and delivers finished work while you're away. Not a summary to review, not a draft to edit — the thing itself gets sorted.
That distinction between work that lands on your desk and w...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Qatar Helium Crisis Threatens AI Supply Chain
item.description:
Hyperscalers are set to spend over a trillion dollars on AI in the next 24 months. Sergey Brin has said on the record he'd rather Google go bankrupt than lose the AI race. Companies are issuing bonds, going negative on free cash flow to build data centers. But all of that investment is now at risk because of a helium gas plant in Qatar.
Here's the core problem. Memory and chips depend on helium — a noble gas with no substitute in chip fabrication. Helium is required during plasma etching to pul...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Skills as Agent Infrastructure Layer
item.description: Anthropic launched skills back in October, and what's happened since then has fundamentally changed how we should think about them. Most people still treat skills as those individual things from the fall. They're not. Skills have gone from personal configuration to organizational infrastructure. Team and enterprise admins are rolling them out company-wide, version-controlled, available in the sidebar, callable inside Excel, PowerPoint, Claude, Copilot. Your organization's methodology no longer l...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Apple's Agentic AI Strategy Revealed
item.description:
Everyone thinks Apple lost the AI race, but what if they've been playing a different game entirely? And what if WWDC is where we finally see it?
I'm not talking about Mac Minis — they're already winning the on-prem AI space for small developers. I'm talking about a neglected software play tied to Apple's massive phone install base, and an opening created by OpenAI stumbling. OpenAI is struggling with their hardware play through Jony Ive, having to refocus because Anthropic is eating into enterp...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Simplify Before Mythos Arrives
item.description:
Claude Mythos just leaked, and this is one of those genuine inflection points. It's the first model we know of trained on Nvidia's GB300 chips. Anthropic has confirmed its existence and given it a new lineage name — Capybara, apparently. It's massive, and it is a step change, not just another 10-15% improvement.
Don't take my word for how powerful it is. Security researchers are saying Mythos is terrifyingly good at finding vulnerabilities. At a conference in San Francisco, one of the most expe...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Stop Wasting AI Tokens
item.description:
The next generation of models — Claude Mythos, whatever OpenAI drops next, the next Gemini — they're coming in one to two months, and they will be significantly more expensive. Trained on pricier chips, priced accordingly. The free ambient intelligence around us will be the dumber models. If you want cutting-edge, you've got to stop burning tokens and blaming the model.
Jensen Huang gave a real number in an interview: $250,000 per individual engineer per year on tokens. You do not want to be th...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Claude Code's 12 Agent Architecture Primitives
item.description:
Anthropic accidentally leaked Claude Code — their two and a half billion dollar run rate product. Most coverage focuses on unreleased feature flags, what ships in the next few weeks. That's fine. I wanted to understand the underlying architecture that sustains this business and extract the baseline infrastructure lessons any team can use to build agents successfully.
Before we get into it, this is the second significant leak from Anthropic in days. Earlier this week, draft blog materials about ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Agents Promise vs Reality
item.description:
Every company is claiming their AI agent does real outcome-focused work — Co-work, Codex, Lindy, Sauna, Google Opal. You can't even keep track of the names. But most of these tools don't solve the hard part about agents, and understanding why points us toward where this is actually going.
Let's start with why this category exists. In January, Anthropic shipped Co-work — an agent that works on your actual computer, opens apps, navigates your browser, does things autonomously. Microsoft panicked,...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agents Need Foundations Not Hype
item.description:
The OpenClaw stories are real. You can build a $320,000 SaaS replacement, a full CRM in days, scale ad creatives from 20 to 2,000. All verified. But what concerns me is people treating this as a blank-slate permission slip — thinking they don't have to worry about data, best practices, or software architecture because OpenClaw will make it better. It won't.
Let me be precise about what OpenClaw actually is: an open-source, self-hosted, model-agnostic AI agent framework. It runs as a persistent ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Agent Infrastructure Stack Decoded
item.description:
Right now, a new infrastructure stack is being assembled for AI agents, with billions in capital behind it. It's not software, it's not agents — it's the layer underneath both that lets agents actually do things in the world. And almost nobody can figure out what's real and what's hype.
We've seen this movie twice before. Between 2006 and 2010, computing shifted from on-prem to the cloud — AWS emerged from that. Between 2012 and 2016, monolithic apps decomposed into microservices. Now we're wat...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Is Collapsing Arbitrage Everywhere
item.description: For thousands of years, since the days of ancient copper merchants on camel caravan routes, the world has been built on arbitrage — gaps between what something costs and what the market pays. These aren't bugs. They're the structure of the economy. The law firm billing ten hours for two hours of thinking. The consultant charging a fortune for synthesizing public data. Offshore dev teams existing because a San Francisco engineer costs more than a Bangalore one. These gaps are the water the econom...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Persistent Agent Lock-In Strategy
item.description:
The most important thing in the Claude code leak isn't the code. Buried in the half-million lines Anthropic accidentally published to a public registry is an unannounced project called Conway — a standalone always-on agent environment. It's not on Anthropic's roadmap. It's internal. And when you line it up against everything else they've shipped this quarter, it reveals a platform strategy most of us aren't seeing clearly enough.
Conway operates as a dedicated sidebar inside the Claude interfac...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI IPOs Raiding Retirement Funds
item.description:
Three companies in the AI space — SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic — collectively valued at around $3 trillion, are planning to go public in the second half of 2026 and early 2027. SpaceX is targeting June, Anthropic is eyeing October, and OpenAI is looking at early 2027. And your retirement account is the exit liquidity.
To understand the scale problem, consider this: every IPO in the United States last year raised a grand total of $47 billion combined. That was the best year in a long time. Spac...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Five Durable Verticals in AI Economy
item.description:
The AI app builder space — Lovable, Replit, Bolt, V0, and the long tail — is collapsing into a commodity. Lovable just raised $330 million at a $6.6 billion valuation with 100,000 new projects created daily. But most of these companies are functionally thin wrappers around Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini. When your product is a UI layer on top of someone else's intelligence, your moat is about a week deep now that Claude Code and Codex exist.
The conventional wisdom says you escape by training your ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Software Solutions to AI's Memory Crisis
item.description:
Google just published one of the most important breakthroughs of the year — TurboQuant. It addresses how LLMs use memory at the core architectural level, and that's a huge deal because intelligence and demand for intelligence are scaling far faster than memory supply.
The comparison everyone's making is to Pied Piper from Silicon Valley the TV show. Pied Piper compressed video files; TurboQuant compresses the KV cache — the memory LLMs use to think. It achieves a 6x memory reduction and up to 8...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Unbundling Management in the AI Era
item.description:
Nearly half of US companies have removed a layer of management in the last year. The pitch is always the same — flatter, leaner, faster, powered by AI — and then everyone's expected to figure out the rest on their own. If things have felt wrong at work since then, you're not imagining it. The company removed something load-bearing.
Managers do three distinct jobs, and understanding this bundle is the key to getting this right. The first is routing — who needs to know what, when. This is informa...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Dark Code Is an Organizational Problem
item.description: Nobody understands their own code anymore. There is code running in production at companies we use every day that nobody can explain — not the engineer who shipped it, not the team that owns the service, not the CTO. The industry is calling this dark code: behavior in production that nobody can trace end to end. It's not buggy code, not spaghetti code, not technical debt. Dark code was never understood by anyone at any point because it was generated by AI, passed automated checks, and shipped. T...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI's Shift from Capability to Economics
item.description:
March 2026 was one of the biggest months in AI history — multiple frontier models shipped, GTC happened, the SaaS apocalypse continued. But I want to read under those headlines and show you the structural moves that happened while everyone was watching model drops. Because the pace is only getting denser, and the skill of pulling structural signal from noise is becoming one of the most valuable things you can practice.
First: inference became a reason to kill product, and Sora was the first vic...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agents Need Your Tacit Knowledge
item.description:
Agents by themselves don't make you productive. Everyone can install an agent in 10 seconds now — that's not the point. Whether you can use it productively is the question that matters, and it turns out to be a very hard problem that almost no company in this space is interested in solving.
The most common message in OpenClaw community forums is simply: 'Now what?' Not 'how do I fix this error' — just 'I got it running and I don't know what to tell it.' There's a structural reason this keeps ha...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Rebuilding the Web for Agents
item.description:
For 50 years, you and I were the most important thing in computing, and we're just not anymore. That's going to change the rest of our lives. But I don't mean that to be discouraging — this is about our agentic partnership with the new intelligence and what a rebuilt world looks like.
Look at any piece of web infrastructure and notice the human affordances baked into the structure. A spreadsheet opens a file because you need to scan rows at the speed your brain processes visual information. A C...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Own Your AI Context
item.description:
Right now all of us are building the most important asset of our careers inside AI systems we don't own, and it's fragmented across ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity. We're feeding in our context, our thinking, our documents — and none of it is portable. We need a bring-your-own-context system for the enterprise worker in 2026, and we have nothing.
The bet Sam Altman and Dario Amodei have been making worked. The fact that we care which AI instance we use is a function of their memory systems. I call ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Auto-Improving Agents for Business
item.description: On March 8th, Andrej Karpathy released a 630-line Python script that created an entirely new paradigm. He pointed an AI agent at his own training code, gave it a single metric to optimize, and went to sleep. Two days later, the agent had run 700 experiments, discovered 20 genuine improvements, and cut training time by 11% on a codebase one of the best ML researchers alive had already optimized for months. It even found a bug in his attention implementation. Not because the agent was smarter, but...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - World Models Need Judgment Boundaries
item.description: Jack Dorsey published a blueprint last week for what's called a world model — a living, always-updated model of everything happening across a company. It got 5 million views in two days. Agency founders are posting implementations, enterprise vendors are rebranding around the concept. And the idea is sound as far as it goes. A huge share of what fills manager calendars — status syncs, alignment meetings, information shuttling — is work that software can do faster and cheaper today. Companies tha...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Proving Value in AI's Generation Era
item.description:
The problem with AI and jobs is that nobody knows what you and I are worth anymore. The standard 2026 career advice — build your portfolio, learn the tools, ship projects — isn't bad, but it leans into the one thing AI makes easy for everyone: generating code. GitHub projects are exploding, App Store listings are exploding. When generation is essentially free, it's incredibly hard to prove you know what you're doing.
This isn't just a junior problem. The entire mechanism by which we prove compe...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Opus 4.7: Directed Optimization Tradeoffs
item.description:
Claude Opus 4.7 is the smartest model Anthropic has ever shipped publicly. It's also the most combative, the most literal, and the first Opus release that costs you measurably more for the same work even though the sticker price didn't move. You have to hold all three of those in view or you'll misread what just happened.
Context matters here. Anthropic shipped 4.7 on the 16th, Claude Design launched the 17th, OpenAI pushed the biggest Codex update since launch the same day, and their next fron...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Write-Time vs Query-Time AI Memory
item.description:
Karpathy's wiki idea got 41,000 bookmarks, and I've gotten hundreds of messages asking whether it makes OpenBrain obsolete. The honest answer is they're fundamentally different systems solving different problems, and the fork between them is the most important architectural decision you can make right now.
Here's what Karpathy got right. Today, every time you ask an AI a question, it rediscovers your knowledge from scratch — finds relevant documents, reads them, synthesizes, gives you an answer...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Computer Use Beats APIs Now
item.description:
OpenAI completely revamped Codex on April 16th, turning it into a desktop agent that operates any Mac application — clicking, typing, running in the background while you work. It's faster and more reliable than Claude's computer use by a wider margin than I expected. And that matters because most enterprise software doesn't have modern APIs, and Codex doesn't need them.
Greg Brockman recently said models have gone from being the product to being part of the product. The brain is effectively bui...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Death of the Mockup-to-Production Handoff
item.description:
Anthropic launched Claude Design and the world fixated on Figma's stock crash. That's the least interesting part of this story. The real story is that Claude Design is the third piece in a coordinated Anthropic stack that's quietly retiring the entire mockup-to-production handoff.
Let me show you the range. You can make pitch decks with live embedded AI — not screenshots, actual working chatbots inside the deck a VC can use in the meeting. Animated product explainer videos rendered in code, so ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Image Generation Joins the Reasoning Stack
item.description:
GPT Image 2 just won 93% of blind pairwise comparisons in Image Arena. The next best model, Google's Nano Banana 2, topped out at 67%. That's a 26-point gap. Leaders in image generation usually trade places by three or four points. This is unprecedented.
But the bigger story is what's underneath that number. Let me make it concrete. Takuya Matsuyama makes a developer note-taking app called Inkdrop. He fed GPT Image 2 a summary of his app, his V6 release notes, and some blog posts about Japanese...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Apple's On-Device AI Gambit
item.description: Tim Cook has stepped down as Apple's CEO, and the coverage has focused on continuity — Apple lifer, smooth handoff. That's accurate, but it misses what the org chart is actually saying. The new CEO, John Turnis, is a hardware engineer. He ran the Mac's transition from Intel to Apple Silicon, arguably the most successful silicon transition any consumer company has pulled off. His second in command, John Suji, who's been running Apple's chip design for a decade, is being elevated to chief hardware...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Workspace Agents Replace Lightweight Automation
item.description:
OpenAI just launched Workspace Agents in ChatGPT, and after spending a week with teams actually trying the research preview, I think the headline is being underplayed. This is not custom GPTs with better connectors. This is a direct competitor to the lightweight automation layer companies have been stitching together with Zapier, Make, N8N, co-pilot Studio, and a lot of internal glue. And the first useful build is not a six-month transformation project — it's probably just an afternoon.
Here's ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Why the Best Model Still Matters
item.description:
GPT 5.5 reset the bar. I think it's the strongest model in the world today, and the most important thing about this release isn't that it's better than 5.4 — it's that it changes what you can reasonably ask a model to do.
The floor moved. That phrase matters because not every release moves the floor. A lot of recent progress has come from inference-time compute — more thinking, more search, more tool calls. That's useful, but it's not the same as the default model being bigger and smarter. 5.5 ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Filtering Agent Launches That Matter
item.description:
Every week another AI agent launches. OpenAI shipped workspace agents, Anthropic put managed agents into beta, Salesforce turned the platform into Headless 360, Perplexity put personal computer on Mac, Moonshot dropped Kimmy K 2.6 with a 300-agent swarm. And if you're leading a team right now, the reaction I keep hearing isn't excitement — it's exhaustion. The question isn't what launched this week. It's which of these things actually deserves an afternoon of your team's attention.
You need a f...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Breaking Corporate AI Tool Inertia
item.description: Your board wants 10x AI results, but the only approved tool can't do your actual job. You know it, your team knows it, but saying it out loud makes you sound like the problem. You're not the problem. There's a false assumption underneath all of this — that AI tools are interchangeable. They are not. A corporate default and a specialist tool can both be AI in the same way a spreadsheet and a data warehouse both have numbers. The category is too broad to tell you whether the tool can do meaningful...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Owning Your Personal AI Stack
item.description: The strangest thing about AI right now is that it's making the computer on your desk important again. For fifteen years, personal computing was the story of the computer disappearing — files moved to the cloud, apps became browser tabs, your OS became a launcher for other people's infrastructure. But agents are changing the direction of travel because a useful agent doesn't just answer a question. It wants to touch the work — read the file, inspect the folder, run the test, edit the spreadsheet,...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Issue Trackers as Agent Infrastructure
item.description:
This is one of the most surprising software stories of 2026. Issue trackers — the most boring software in the engineering stack, the thing everyone complains about — are quietly becoming one of the most important pieces of agent infrastructure. They weren't designed for AI. They became useful because they happened to encode something agents desperately need: durable state, ownership, permissions, history, and a clean idea of what should happen next.
If you want agents to do real work, the hard ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Buyer-Agent Economy Replaces Seller Funnels
item.description:
Stripe announced a massive set of new agent commerce products, and everyone's buzzing about the demo where an AI agent buys you coffee. That's not the point. The point is that power in the entire internet economy is shifting from the seller to the buyer for the first time in decades. This is the biggest change in commerce patterns in twenty years.
Let me walk through three shifts underneath this announcement. First, why the old funnel was really a machine for making human intent visible. Second...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Auditing Your Job for Durability
item.description: The first sign your job is on thin ice is often a full calendar and no clue what's actually happening. Your manager's happy, the work's getting done, but less and less of it truly needs you. That's exactly what happened to travel agents. Expedia didn't erase the profession overnight. Online booking changed the economics of routine work first, and nothing visibly changed. The real break came later when downturns forced the industry to admit what had already shifted. I think a lot of knowledge wor...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - The Consumer AI Anticipation Gap
item.description:
The main problem in AI right now is that software is finally capable enough to help, and somehow it has become one more thing to manage. I don't need another chatbot. I don't need another blank box waiting for me to invent the perfect prompt. I don't need another agent that says it can do anything and then sits there waiting for me to assign it work. And I definitely don't need my attention sucked into managing a fleet of agents. That's not what an assistant does. That's a new inbox.
The fronti...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Semantic Work Primitives for AI Agents
item.description:
This is about the strategy we need as product leaders when thinking about where agents play best. I'm asserting that the work primitive is what really matters—not the agent's ability to use a computer. A lot of us assume that computer use levels the playing field, but there's a deeper strategy that needs to be more widely understood.
When an AI agent opens a browser, clicks buttons, fills forms, checks your calendar—it feels like the model has crossed a line. But the visible work is distracting...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Durable Agent Workflows Beyond Model Lock-in
item.description: After April 2026, OpenClaw looks less like a viral agent demo and more like a real runtime that gets real work done. Peter and team shipped at an exhausting pace — task updates, memory updates, provider updates, channel updates — and the shape of the product fundamentally changed. It's no longer just a model with hands. It's becoming an action layer for agents with durable multi-step orchestration, state management, retry behaviors, and proper channel delivery across Slack, Discord, Teams, and m...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - End of Trusted Human Code
item.description:
The heart of this story is Mozilla's Mythos experiment and how it changes how we view human code. We've always assumed human code is the gold standard and AI code isn't great. But now in 2026, things are different. Some of the most respected engineers at the most respected institutions are suggesting AI code may become more trusted than human code.
The most important thing about Mythos is not that AI found bugs in Firefox. It's that it makes the sentence 'a good human engineer wrote this' feel ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Packaging Work Beyond Prompts
item.description:
When you look at what makes an AI agent actually effective, it's not the model's raw intelligence — it's the scaffolding around it. Think of it as a mech suit. The model is inside, but the suit is what lets it do real work. And most people have no clear mental model of what that scaffolding consists of. So let me lay it out.
A prompt is what you use when you want to do something once. It's temporary, moment-specific, and doesn't carry tools, permissions, or reusability. The problem is most peop...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agents Break Enterprise Procurement
item.description: Twenty dollars and two hours. That's what it took an autonomous agent with no credentials and zero insider help to get full read and write access to Lily, the AI platform used daily by 70% of McKinsey's 40,000 consultants. Access to tens of millions of chat messages, tens of thousands of user accounts, every system prompt governing how the platform reasons—all writable. An attacker could have silently rewritten how the AI advises consultants who advise the largest firms in the world. The startup...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - LLM-as-Judge for Agent Control
item.description:
I've told you stories about agents deleting production data, sending unauthorized emails, spending money they shouldn't have. These are real incidents. What we aren't talking about is the pattern that's emerged in the last couple of months that actually prevents this. Consider this a PSA.
I'm not talking about jailbreaks or hallucinations. I'm talking about the agent doing what you trained it to do — but going past where it had permission. Inferring authorization from a thread that didn't grant...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agentic Commerce Responsibility Wars
item.description:
Your agent is about to spend your money — your card, your company's, your customers'. Real money is going into agents' hands in 2026 without a human present. And when it moves to the wrong place, somebody's going to be left holding the bag. Right now, six protocol camps are fighting over who that somebody is.
There are six layers to an agentic purchase: who decides where the agent shops, what counts as proof it was allowed to act, who owns the credential when payment fires, which rails carry th...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Memory Beyond Vector Search
item.description: Pinecone is a vector database company, and last month they shipped a product that basically says vector search is not enough on its own. That's a strange thing for a vector database company to say. And they're not alone. SAP spent over a billion euros on AI infrastructure — none of it for chatbots or language models. Google made knowledge architecture the headline at Cloud Next. Cloudflare shipped a memory product for agents. Microsoft keeps pushing graphs for AI. Every serious infrastructure ve...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Implementation Layer Wars in Enterprise AI
item.description:
Let me tell you why I'm excited about the implementation challenge for agents. People think this is an agent story. It's actually three stories converging: finance changing what it believes about the future of software, hyperscalers discovering what doesn't work, and companies figuring out where the disproportionate value in AI actually lives. All these forces are converging on a private equity-driven services deployment model.
Private equity has long had this saying — SaaS companies all taste ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agentic Pricing Replaces SaaS Seats
item.description: Salesforce just reported AgentForce hit $800 million run rate in ARR, up 169% year-over-year, with 2.4 billion agentic work units processed. Not tokens — work units. They're counting the actions agents complete inside the platform and billing accordingly. Same week, Microsoft Agent 365 went generally available at $15 per user per month with a governance license that manages how agents act inside your Microsoft environment. If you're building agents that touch Salesforce or Microsoft systems, the...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Agents Reshaping Real Work
item.description: A guy recovered five Bitcoin — about $400,000 — from a wallet he'd locked himself out of eleven years ago. Not with a better password cracker, but with Claude. He uploaded files from his old college hard drive, Claude sorted through a decade of forgotten folders, found an older wallet.dat file from before the password change, matched it with a mnemonic recovery phrase he still had, and the wallet opened. That's illustrative of where we are. The model launches keep happening, but the interesting ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Workflow-First AI Investment Logic
item.description:
Gartner says more than 40% of agentic AI projects will get killed by 2027 — cost, unclear business value, inadequate risk controls. I've seen all of these firsthand. But the issue is not the agentic tech. The issue is how people are making investment decisions.
Here's the first thing going wrong. AI investment is not an AI question. It's a question about the shape of your work. The model question is downstream of that. The vendor question is downstream of that. What sits at the root is how the ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Marketing in the Interpretation Economy
item.description:
The internet economy is fundamentally changing, and we're not paying enough attention. For 25 years, everything has been built on attention — get eyeballs, get clicks, sell ads, push people through the funnel. That era is ending. We're moving into what I call the interpretation economy, where the entire web is filtered through what an AI thinks about you.
Someone is going to ask about you — not your company, you individually — whether they should trust you. And they won't ask you. They'll ask a...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Protocol Stack Explained
item.description: Google IO opens today, and there will be a ton of agent demos. But the more interesting story is what's happening underneath — specifically the protocols Google is pushing to drive the agentic revolution. Six agent protocols have launched in the last year: MCP, A2A, AG-UI, A2UI, AP2, and X42. If you're building an AI agent product, that list is hard to wrestle with. New acronyms keep popping up, new diagrams, new claims that some missing piece of the agent stack has been solved. Here's my read: ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Infrastructure Layer Controls AI Agents
item.description:
The companies that get to decide whether your agent actually ships are probably not the ones you're thinking of. Not OpenAI, not Anthropic. I'm talking about Cloudflare, Stripe, Auth0, Okta, Snowflake, Datadog. None of these companies build models. None are on most teams' AI stack roadmaps. But all of them are going to decide whether your AI agent gets deployed in production.
The model is one piece of the agent economy. The infrastructure companies — the ones that determine where the agent runs...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Prompting AI as Senior Partner
item.description:
Prompt engineering is dead as a differentiator. It's table stakes now. You need it, but you don't get credit for it. What matters in 2026, especially after Opus 4.7 and OpenAI 5.5, is what comes on top of prompt engineering. And most people haven't made this transition.
The labs will tell you to just ask AI for what you want. That's only true if people know what they want. And the more complex the agentic workflow, the harder it is to articulate that. These agents are a hundred times more power...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Data Rooms Prevent AI Hallucinations
item.description:
A few weeks ago, Sullivan and Cromwell — one of the most prestigious law firms on the planet — had to write an apology letter to a federal bankruptcy judge. Their emergency motion had been filed with dozens of fabricated or misquoted citations. AI hallucinations. The other side's lawyers caught them. Sullivan and Cromwell's own review did not. The partner who signed the apology is the co-head of the firm's restructuring practice.
This is the failure mode I want you to think about. I'm not talki...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Safety Is System Design
item.description: Emergence AI built a virtual town, populated it with AI agents, and let them run for 15 days. Not one prompt, not one task — a long-running experiment. The agents had names, roles, memory, relationships, laws, energy needs, and tools. They could vote, write proposals, earn resources. Importantly, they could also steal, intimidate, fight, and set buildings on fire. Emergence ran five identical versions: one with Claude agents, one Gemini, one Grok, one ChatGPT mini, and one mixed-model town. Same...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Supply Chain Changes Everything
item.description: On Microsoft's Q3 earnings call, Satya Nadella told investors the company will spend $190 billion on capex this calendar year and still expects to be capacity constrained through year end. The most valuable software company on the planet cannot get enough capacity to meet its own demand. And when he says capacity constrained, he doesn't mean Microsoft ran out of GPUs. The supply problem is a layer below — it's whether you can manufacture enough chips packaged with the memory they need for modern...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Platform Teams Under Agentic Pressure
item.description:
My name is Emma. I joined OpenAI in 2023 to lead the data platform infrastructure engineering group. We handle everything underneath — big data, streaming, ML infra, feature stores, training data pipelines, eval data. Every team in the company sits on top of us, from product to research to finance to HR.
In the last six months, things have really accelerated. A year ago it wasn't that different from traditional software engineering. Now with Codex and rapidly improving models, we're seeing radi...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Public AI Work Closes Apprenticeship Gap
item.description: Shopify's internal coding agent is named River. In one 30-day stretch this spring, nearly 6,000 employees used it across more than 4,400 Slack channels. In a single week, River opened 1,800 pull requests in Shopify's main monorepo. About one in every eight merged pull requests at Shopify now comes from River. Those are big numbers, and they're what most people grabbed onto when Toby Lütke posted about this. But underneath those numbers is a design choice that is actually the story: River doesn't...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Building Trustworthy AI Office Documents
item.description:
Every tool can now build you a PowerPoint or Excel file in minutes. Claude builds them, ChatGPT analyzes them, Copilot for Office hit general availability in April. What none of them give you is a way to know the file is correct, accurate, and complete.
I lived this last quarter. I opened an Excel financial model — assumption inputs at the top, revenue projections, valuation outputs rolling up cleanly. A written guide said it had been validated. Then I opened the revenue growth row and the form...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Analytics as Product Rudder
item.description:
We've never had the chance to observe the impact of our decisions in real time the way we can with agents. And instead of seeing this as an opportunity, we treat it as a burden — more evals, more telemetry, more observability. But this isn't a technical problem. It's the chance to shape and steer agents running at full speed, if we get good signal back.
When I tell people I've had the equivalent of 55,000 developer years of code production in the last year — about 10 billion tokens — maybe you ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Post-Prototype Product Management
item.description:
So much of what I see in PM training right now is around prototyping — PMs using Lovable, Claude Code, Codex. That's fine, but it's table stakes. It is not the heart of where product is going. AI makes generation easier, which moves the bottleneck, and we need to be very intentional about where we apply scarce human judgment from here.
Here's the real picture. Microsoft has built more than one million Power Platform assets internally — 18,000 agent environments, 170,000 Power Apps, 50,000 autom...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Assembling Context Windows Locally
item.description:
People keep asking how I use AI, so I'm going to start sharing what's surprising or interesting each week. This week, the big thing is assembling context windows on your local file system with Codex — and I mean Codex specifically, not Claude Code or Claude Co-worker. I've tried this same workflow with both. It doesn't work.
What I do now for serious work is tell Codex to look at my file system and find files using natural language. I don't name the file or reference a specific title — I descri...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Proving Judgment in the AI Age
item.description:
Microsoft says 86% of us treat AI output as a starting point, not a final answer. Good — that's what we should be doing. They also say 58% of AI users are producing work they couldn't have produced a year ago, rising to over 80% among advanced users. The obvious story is that AI makes people more productive. That's true, but it's not the real problem. The deeper problem is AI makes more people *look* productive, and the old evidence no longer carries the same signal.
A memo can be polished, a p...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Harnesses Matter More Than Models
item.description:
Everyone is getting the Opus 4.8 story wrong. We're used to the 2025 playbook — new model drops, new high bar, talk about what it unlocks. We're in a different stage of the race now. 4.8 is by some measures the strongest model out there, but that no longer means it's the most useful model for you.
First, let's get the elephant out of the room. 4.8 is not the big model drop everyone's waiting for from Anthropic. Everyone's waiting for Mythos. The real reason 4.8 dropped on May 28th is that Anthr...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Token Dashboards as Intelligence Compasses
item.description:
I built a token burn dashboard, and yes, I burned about 800 million tokens last Thursday. But the point isn't the number — it's what the dashboard reveals about how you use AI and where your imagination falls short.
The core idea is simple: if you don't have a feedback loop showing how you deploy AI, you can't improve. Token burn is the best proxy we have because it's easy to measure and it's correlated with intelligence and successful outcomes. Study after study from the major labs shows that ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Four Types of AI Layoffs
item.description: We're getting the AI layoffs story wrong. 'AI layoffs' has become a generic label slapped on everything, and it's obscuring what's actually happening. These are not one phenomenon — they're at least four distinct categories, each telling you something very different about the company doing them. If you're a leader, a layoff announcement is the highest-stakes strategy signal a company can send. It's free competitive intelligence. If you're a job seeker, understanding which type you're looking at ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Literacy Beyond Code
item.description:
Everyone's asking whether Claude Code or Codex is better. Wrong question. The better question is: what does each tool make you better at doing with agents? Because the skill of 2026 is agent literacy. Here's my shorthand: Claude makes steering agents feel natural. Codex makes dispatching agents feel natural. That difference may matter more than which model wins a benchmark this month, because it teaches you a habit.
This is like the Mac versus Windows fight of the agent age — not because one ma...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Apple's AI Surface vs GPU Cloud
item.description: Apple used WWDC to show Siri AI, confirm the Google Gemini collaboration, and expand private cloud compute into Google Cloud using Nvidia GPUs. Those sound like three separate headlines, but they're really Apple answering one question: when AI starts doing real work for you all day long, where does that work run? In a chatbot tab? In a giant cloud service burning tokens and GPU time? Or inside the computer you already bought — the iPhone, the Mac, the apps, the files, the photos, the messages — ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Codex as Computing Paradigm Shift
item.description: I'm obsessed with Codex right now, and not because it writes code. The name is misleading — it sounds like a developer tool, but what it actually does is make your computer feel like something you can hand work to. My files, browser, folders, drafts, screenshots — Codex moves across all of it. Before Codex, my AI work was still basically chat: draft this, summarize that, help me think. With Codex, I started giving my computer jobs. Find the transcript, read the folder, compare versions, render t...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Frontier AI as National Security Asset
item.description: This is unprecedented. The US government just ordered Anthropic to take Fable 5 offline, blocking access for foreign governments, foreign companies, foreign individuals, and even foreign nationals inside the United States. That last part is what makes this much stranger than a normal export control story, because for a modern AI company that sells globally, employs globally, and runs through international cloud infrastructure, the practical way to comply is to shut off the models for everyone. A...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Harness Layer vs Token Economics
item.description:
OpenAI and Anthropic are both moving toward IPOs, and most conversation will collapse into whether these companies are worth the numbers people are putting on them. I think that's the least useful place to start. The better question is: what are public investors actually being asked to believe?
The answer is pretty simple. They're being asked to believe these companies can do two things simultaneously. One, make intelligence cheap enough to serve at massive scale. Two, build the layer around th...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Buildout Versus Bubble Sorting
item.description: AI stocks are correcting and the story is evolving in real time. Big names are selling off, Broadcom reports record AI revenue and still gets punished, and the market keeps asking: was this whole AI trade just a bubble? The hyperscalers are on pace to spend around $700 billion this year on AI infrastructure. Some are raising debt, power is tight, memory is expensive, and inside most companies the clean ROI story isn't fully there yet. So if you want to say this looks like a bubble, I get it. But...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Maintenance Over Agent Building
item.description:
Vercel made its sales agent better by deleting 80% of its tools. That sentence sounds wrong if you've been following the hype, where agents supposedly get better as you give them more stuff — more context, more memory, more integrations, more autonomy. Vercel's example is a healthy counterexample.
What they did was study one of their best reps. They watched the actual workflow — what did the rep ignore, what made a lead real, what research happened before a reply, when was a message actually a ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Portable Procedures for AI Agents
item.description: A few weeks ago I made the Open Brain video arguing that agents need access to your context — your projects, decisions, people, prior work — in a way that's not trapped inside one app. That struck a chord. But there's a second problem that shows up the moment you solve memory: even if the agent knows what you know, it still doesn't know how you work. You still have to say 'when you research, don't trust stale model memory.' You still have to say 'when you write, don't sound like a generic AI new...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Trust Stacks for Synthetic Media
item.description:
Someone could clone my voice today. Not in some speculative future — today. There's enough clean audio of me talking about AI infrastructure and enterprise data out there that the tools could produce something convincing. And I know because people have tried.
So let me do three things here. First, I played a synthetic clone of my voice, clearly labeled. It was impressive and frankly a little creepy. And that tension is the whole point.
Voice cloning is already very good if you have clean sourc...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Ownership Over Agent Building
item.description: The fastest way to make an AI agent dangerous is to let everyone use it and nobody own it. And I think we've made agents sound way more confusing than they need to be. People hear 'agent' and think fully autonomous digital employee. The brand name doesn't matter — ChatGPT, Claude, Codex, Cursor — they can all be agents. What matters is whether you've delegated a job to it. If you ask ChatGPT a question and move on, that's an assistant interaction. But if you have a custom GPT that reads your not...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Anthropic's Hidden Lead Over OpenAI
item.description:
OpenAI looks like it won the week — rumors of GPT-5.6, hiring Noam Shazeer, one of the original authors of Attention Is All You Need, while Anthropic's Fable model got banned and negotiations with Washington stalled. But I want to tell a different story. Anthropic also landed major talent that didn't make headlines: John Jumper, a Nobel Prize winner for his AlphaFold work with Demis Hassabis, left Google for Anthropic. Google had a terrible week.
The talent movements matter far more than the Fa...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Surveillance Gaps, Remuneration Reforms, Crypto Regime
item.description:
Good morning. Let's get into today's compliance briefing, starting with the most operationally significant development for wholesale markets teams.
The 2026 edition of eflow's Global Trends in Market Abuse and Trade Surveillance report is now live, drawing on enforcement data and input from over 300 regulatory professionals. The headline numbers demand attention. Fifty percent of all fines issued globally were related to trade surveillance system and controls failures. That's not insider dealin...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - SMCR Reform and Regulatory Pipeline Priorities
item.description:
Good morning. The most consequential development for compliance teams right now is the coordinated SMCR reform push from HM Treasury, the FCA, and the PRA, all three publishing consultation papers on 15 July 2025. The consultation window closes 7 October 2025, and if you haven't already started your firm's response, now is the time.
Let's start with what matters most for senior managers and certification. The FCA and PRA are proposing what they're calling Phase 1 reforms — changes that can be m...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Handbook Upgrades and Regulatory Housekeeping
item.description:
Good morning. Today's compliance briefing is relatively light on headline enforcement or market abuse developments, but there are a few housekeeping and strategic items worth noting.
First, the FCA has formally launched its redesigned Handbook website. This is part of the regulator's stated 5-year strategy to become a smarter regulator. The new site improves navigation, makes it easier to understand connections between rules, and critically, allows users to compare different versions of Handboo...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - SMCR Reform and Enforcement Escalation
item.description: Let's start with the biggest structural development. The UK government is undertaking the most sweeping review of the Senior Managers and Certification Regime since its introduction. This is playing out in three phases, and compliance teams need to understand all of them. Phase One, driven by the FCA and PRA through consultation papers CP25/21 and CP18/25, is essentially a streamlining exercise. The 12-week temporary cover rule is being revised so that firms must submit an SMF application within...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK Reporting Overhaul and SMCR Reform
item.description:
Let's get into the key developments that matter for compliance teams across wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions.
The biggest news this month is the FCA's launch of CP25/32, a consultation proposing a fundamental overhaul of the UK's onshore MiFIR transaction reporting regime. If implemented, the changes would reduce the number of reportable fields, limit mandatory reporting to instruments traded on UK venues, remove FX derivatives from scope entirely, and shorten the defaul...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK MAR Compliance Framework Essentials
item.description: Let's start with what matters most for trading desks and compliance teams today. The FCA continues to signal that preventing, detecting, and punishing market abuse is among its highest enforcement priorities, directly tied to its statutory objectives of consumer protection, market integrity, and competition. The enforcement toolkit is formidable: unlimited fines for civil breaches of UK MAR, and on the criminal side under the Criminal Justice Act 1993 and Financial Services Act 2012, custodial s...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Enforcement, MAR Actions, Algorithmic Trading
item.description:

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Remuneration Reform, Market Abuse, SMCR Accountability
item.description: Let's start with the most consequential development for wholesale markets compliance teams this period. The FCA and PRA have jointly published their final policy statement on remuneration reform for dual-regulated firms — banks, building societies, and PRA-designated investment firms. These changes took effect on 16 October 2025, and some may be applied optionally to ongoing performance years or unvested awards from prior years. item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Market Abuse and SMCR Accountability
item.description:

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Insider Dealing Fines and SMCR Reporting Relief
item.description: Let's start with enforcement. The FCA has fined Russell Gerrity just over £309,000 for insider dealing. Gerrity was a consultant working on oil rig decommissioning projects and used confidential information about planned corporate transactions to trade shares ahead of public announcements, making profits of roughly £129,000. This is a textbook inside information case, but what matters for compliance teams is the FCA's methodology: they built the case through detailed analysis of information acce...

item.description:

item.description: Today's item is a brief one, but it's worth flagging. Now, the substantive detail available from the published page is limited — it's essentially a placeholder on GOV.UK signalling that this consultation exists — but the framing matters. item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - AI Governance and SM&CR Accountability
item.description:

Let's start with the headline message from the FCA. CEO Nikhil Rathi confirmed in December 2025 that the FCA will not introduce AI-specific rules. His reasoning is straightforward — the technology evolves every three to six months, making prescriptive regulation impractical. Instead, the FCA is rel...

But for compliance teams at investment banks, the action to watch closely is Dinosaur Merchant Bank, fined three hundred and t...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - SM&CR Reform and Regulatory Grid Update
item.description: The most significant development for compliance teams today is the FCA's publication of Consultation Paper CP25/21, which proposes substantial reforms to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime. This is a two-phase effort. Phase one targets immediate practical improvements — greater flexibility around the 12-week rule for temporary senior management function cover, extended validity periods for criminal record checks in SMF applications, streamlined certification processes to reduce duplica...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK MAR Post-Brexit Surveillance Expectations
item.description:

The headline point is straightforward but operationally significant: UK MAR now stands as an independent regime, separate from EU MAR. For Global Banking and Markets divisions operating across jurisdictions, this means dual compliance obligations are a reality, not a theoretical concern. Firms must ensure th...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - PRA FCA Remuneration Rules Overhaul
item.description:

The PRA and FCA have announced sweeping reforms to senior banker pay rules, effective October 16, 2025. These changes apply to 2025 pay awards and any outstanding payments, so the implementation window is essentially now.

item.description:

Let's start with market abuse. ESMA published a consultation on 19 February proposing amendments to its MAR guidelines on delayed disclosure of inside information. item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA PRA Joint Enforcement Action
item.description: Today's briefing is necessarily brief given limited source material, but there's a point worth flagging. The FCA and PRA jointly fined R. Raphael & Sons plc — a case that, while dating back to 2019, serves as a useful reminder of how the dual-regulator model operates in practice when it comes to enforcement. Joint fines from both the FCA and PRA signal that where a firm falls under both supervisory regimes, neither regulator is content to defer to the other. Both will pursue action independently...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Banker Bonus Rules Relaxed Immediately
item.description:
Good morning. Today's key development for compliance teams at UK investment banks concerns a significant loosening of the remuneration rules for Material Risk Takers at dual-regulated firms. On 16 October 2025, the FCA and PRA published a joint policy statement that went further than their original consultation proposals, and the changes took effect immediately.
Let's get into the substance. The most impactful change is to deferral periods. Previously, Senior Management Function holders faced d...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK Enforcement Decline Hits Financial Regulation
item.description:
Today's briefing focuses on a significant and broad-based decline in UK regulatory enforcement in 2025, with direct implications for compliance teams across wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions.
Let's start with the headline numbers from the Financial Conduct Authority. FCA penalties dropped to £196 million in 2025, down from approximately £800 million the prior year — a fall of nearly £600 million. Half as many firms were subject to enforcement action compared to 2024. Now,...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Enforcement Trends and Market Abuse Evolution
item.description:

Let's start with the most consequential theme: individual accountability is now the defining feature of regulatory enforcement on both sides of the Atlantic. item.description:
The headline development today is the FCA and PRA jointly confirming concrete changes to the Senior Managers and Certification Regime, marking the conclusion of the Phase 1 reforms that were first proposed in July 2025. The regulators are framing this as a meaningful step toward halving the overall regulatory burden SMCR places on firms, and for compliance teams at wholesale banks this has immediate practical implications.
